questions.description: Barry collects old and unique comic books and graphic novels as a hobby.
questions.text: Barry collects old and unique comic books and graphic novels as a hobby. On Monday Barry's friend Stacey phones him to ask whether he would be interested in having a collection of old comic books she has found in her attic, as otherwise she "would just throw them out in the recycling". Barry is delighted and immediately agrees to take the comics. When he collects the comics the next day Barry is so happy with them that he promises he will pay Stacey €200 for them when he gets his wages on Wednesday. Stacey is delighted by this and in the knowledge that she will get €200 on Wednesday she goes out for an expensive dinner on Monday night. However, when Barry is paid on Wednesday he does not give Stacey any money, and Stacey is now threatening to sue him for the €200. On Thursday morning Barry sees an advertisement in a local paper which states:"Limited Time Offer: Early Edition Copy of "Superman" for €50. If interested email Rebecca at comicgeek@email.ie by close of business Thursday" Barry decides that this would be a good addition to his comic book collection, so on Thursday he sends an email to Rebecca to say he is interested in buying the comic. Unfortunately he sends the email in such a hurry that he accidentally says he will pay €500 for the comic. Rebecca immediately replies saying that she accepts his offer to pay €500 for the comic book. Rebecca is now saying that they have a contract, and that Barry must pay her €500 for the comic book. Barry says that Rebecca must have known it was a mistake and that the comic book is not worth more than €50. Advise Barry on his legal position vis à vis (a) Stacey and (b) Rebecca

questions.description: In June 2010 Charles wins the 10 kilometre race at the Irish National… Misrepresent, Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: In June 2010 Charles wins the 10 kilometre race at the Irish National Indoor Cycling Championships. After his win he takes part in a national radio interview in which he says that he is "looking forward to competing in the 2012 Olympics in London". The next month he enters into a sponsorship contract with a large Irish sports company, Megasports Ltd, whereby Megasports Ltd agree to pay Charles €15,000 per year five years if he appears in their advertising campaigns and wears their logo on his cycling clothes and cycling helmet when he competes. After signing the contract, Charles features in a television advertisement for Megasports Ltd which shows him cycling past a large mock signpost to "London". However, in 2012 Charles is so outraged by the level of homelessness in London that he refuses to compete in the 2012 Olympic games as a measure of protest. Megasports Ltd is furious as its managing director, Tom, says the company only sponsored Charles because Tom heard his radio interview in September 2010 and assumed he would compete in the 2012 Olympics. He says that Charles misrepresenented his position both in the radio interview and by taking part in the television advertisement in which he was seen cycling in the direction of London. In addition, when Charles competed at the French Open Indoor Cycling Championships in January 2012 he is prevented by new competition rules, introduced in 2011, from wearing the Megasports Ltd logo on his helmet and cycling clothes. Megasports Ltd contacts Charles to say that it considers this a breach of the sponsorship contract. Megasports Ltd is threatening to stop its sponsorship of Charles and to sue Charles for misrepresentation and breach of contract. Advise Charles.

questions.description: John is a plumber who specialises in the supply and installation of… Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: John is a plumber who specialises in the supply and installation of bathroom equipment such as showers, baths and Jacuzzis. Last June John entered into a contract with Niamh, whereby John agreed to supply and install a Jacuzzi three meters long and one metre deep in Niamh's bathroom, at a cost of €2,000, the full amount to be paid on 1 October. However, when the Jacuzzi is installed, Niamh is annoyed because it is only two and a half meters long and ninety centimetres deep. Other than the difference in size, the Jacuzzi is perfectly useable and has all the features Niamh requires. Niamh believes that John is in breach of contract and refuses to pay John the €2,000. In addition she wants damages of €3,000 to cover the cost of tearing out the Jacuzzi that has been installed and replacing it with a properly installed, bigger Jacuzzi and another €2,000 for the stress and inconvenience of getting the Jacuzzi replaced. However, John says the difference in size of the Jacuzzi is only minor, and that the Jacuzzi installed is still worth at least €1,750. He suspects that Niamh has no intention of actually replacing the installed Jacuzzi but instead wants to use the money to go on holidays. Advise John on (a) whether he is in breach of contract and (b) assuming John is in breach of contract, whether Niamh has to pay the €2,000 for the work done and (c) assuming John is in breach of contract, the extent to which Niamh will be successful in claiming damages.

questions.description: Ann is an actress who agrees to play the part of a prostitute in a new… Consideration, Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Ann is an actress who agrees to play the part of a prostitute in a new film Pretty Girl. The film features many graphic sex scenes and nudity, and is banned by censors in several countries. Even though the film is not banned in Ireland it is never shown in Irish cinemas, although a small number of DVDs of the film are sold from sex shops. The film ends up making a loss, and the film producer, Clive is now refusing to pay Ann, claiming that that the contract is unenforceable as it is immoral and against public policy. Ann is finding it difficult to get another acting job in Ireland, so she decides to sell her car to make some money. On Monday she contacts Declan, a local car dealer, to discuss the possible purchase of her car. He promises to pay Ann €2,000 for the car, payment upon delivery. Ann is delighted and promises to deliver the car the following Friday. However, the next day Declan phones Ann to inform her that he has changed his mind as he has too many second hand cars in stock already. He claims that as the offer was a unilateral one and she has not yet performed "her part of the deal" or provided any consideration, he is entitled to do so. Ann wants to sue Declan for breach of contract. Advise Ann on her legal position vis à vis (a) Clive [and]{.underline} (b) Declan.

questions.description: Answer (a) [and]{.underline} (b) (a) Explain the ways in which an exclusion clause can be incorporated into a contract.
questions.text: Answer (a) [and]{.underline} (b) (a) Explain the ways in which an exclusion clause can be incorporated into a contract. [And]{.underline} (b) Explain whether it is possible to exclude for fundamental breach of contract in Ireland.

questions.description: Answer (a) [and]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the duty of a plaintiff to… Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Answer (a) [and]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the duty of a plaintiff to mitigate loss caused by a breach of contract. [And]{.underline} (b) Discuss the difference between a liquidated damages clause and a penalty clause.

questions.description: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the impact of EU law initiatives on Irish contract law.
questions.text: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the impact of EU law initiatives on Irish contract law. [Or]{.underline} (b) Explain the duty of utmost good faith in Irish insurance contract law.

questions.description: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the circumstances in which a contract will be declared invalid on the…
questions.text: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the circumstances in which a contract will be declared invalid on the grounds of economic duress. Or (b) In Grogan v Cooke (1812) 7 Ball & B. 234 Manners LC stated: "if there be a fair and bona fide consideration the Court will not enter minutely into it, and see that it is full and ample." Discuss the relationship between this principle and the law of undue influence.

questions.description: Inspired by the Olympics, Ross decides to exercise more and to become… Consideration, Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Inspired by the Olympics, Ross decides to exercise more and to become stronger and fitter. On Monday he sees the following advertisement on a website: "Second-hand treadmill for sale. All offers over €300 considered. If interested please email LauraK@email.ie". Ross emails Laura to tell her he is very interested in buying the treadmill, but is only willing to pay €250 for it. Laura immediately replies, saying that she will not sell the treadmill for less than €300, but that she will not sell it to anyone else before Saturday to give Ross time to think about it. On Friday afternoon Ross emails Laura to say he will pay €300 for the treadmill "as agreed". However Laura informs Ross that she already sold the treadmill to another individual, Ben, that morning and so cannot sell it to Ross. Ross wants to know if he has entered into a binding contract for the sale of the treadmill and whether he has a cause of action against Laura for selling the treadmill to Ben before Saturday. On Tuesday Ross hears that Sam, a friend of one of his work colleagues, is selling his mountain bike. After a brief discussion Ross promises to pay Sam €200 for the bike, payment upon delivery, and Sam promises to deliver the bike the following Saturday. However on Wednesday Sam phones Ross to tell him he has changed his mind and no longer wants to sell his bike. He claims that as the offer was a unilateral one and Ross has not yet performed "his part of the deal" or provided any consideration, he is entitled to do so. Ross wants to sue Sam for breach of contract. Finally, on Wednesday Ross sees the following advertisement in a sports magazine: "Mighty Muscle Protein Drink: Get Strong as You Train! Drink Mighty Muscle twice a day and feel the difference! Guaranteed results after two weeks or we'll pay you €500!" Excited by the advertisement, Ross buys a month's supply of Might Muscle and takes it as directed. However, after two weeks it has had no effect. When he contacts Mighty Muscle Ltd they refuse to pay him €500, saying that the advertisement was not a serious offer, and even if there was an offer, Ross did not accept it. Ross wants to know if he can claim his €500. Advise Ross on his legal position vis à vis (a) Laura [and]{.underline} (b) Sam [and]{.underline} (c) Mighty Muscle Ltd.

questions.description: Caoimhe owns a hairdressing salon which was located in the centre of a…
questions.text: Caoimhe owns a hairdressing salon which was located in the centre of a midlands town, next door to a car park. She advertises the sale of the business and premises in the local newspaper. Alan is looking to open up his own hairdressing salon, and seeing the advertisement, he visits the premises. Caoimhe tells him that the profits of the business are usually about €30,000 per year and that she anticipates that profits will increase by at least 15% in the next year. She offers Alan the opportunity to look at the business accounts, but Alan says he will take her word for it because he is not very good at maths. The day after Alan's visit, the local authority notifies Caoimhe that in three months' time the High Street will become a traffic free zone and the car park next to the salon will be closed. Caoimhe decides not to pass this information to Alan because she knows that her customers rely on being able to park close to the salon. The following week Alan enters into a written contract to purchase the premises for €200,000. The contract makes no mention of the profit figures or the proposed closing of the car park. After taking over the premises, Alan spends €5,000 on new internal fittings for the salon. A month later, Alan asks his accountant to read Caoimhe's accounts and discovers that they show that profits had not exceeded €20,000 in any of the last three years and were on a downward trend. Two months later the High Street becomes a traffic free zone, causing a drastic reduction in trade as clients of the salon can no longer park nearby. As a result Alan wishes he had never bought the salon. Advise Alan on whether he has any cause of action against Caoimhe [and]{.underline} on the remedies that may be available to him.

questions.description: Steve is a supplier of oats and grains to the owners of the horses and… Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Steve is a supplier of oats and grains to the owners of the horses and other livestock. Steve sells 520 sacks of oats to Rory, a racehorse trainer. Rory tells Steve that the oats are for his racehorses. They agree in a hastily written contract that the oats are to be delivered in 52 weekly instalments of 10 sacks each. Rory agrees to pay for each instalment of oats as they are delivered. The written agreement makes no mention of the quality of oats. The first nine instalments of oats are delivered without any problems arising. However after eating the tenth instalments of oats Rory's horses become ill and require veterinary treatment. As a result of the illness the racehorse cannot be entered into races for six months. The vet confirms that oats delivered had been contaminated while in storage at Steve's warehouse. Rory is now suing Steve for breach of contract, arguing that the oats were of poor quality and unsuitable for his racehorses. Rory seeks damages, including the veterinary fees and the loss of profits during the time the racehorses could not be entered into races. Rory claims that his horses were in prime condition and would have won all their races. In addition, Rory has refused to accept delivery of any further instalments of oats from Steve since his racehorses became ill. He says that as a result of what occurred he considers the contract at an end and will not take delivery of any further instalments of oats from Steve. The agreed price under the contract was €10 a sack. Rory is now paying €20 per sack from an alternative supplier. Advise Steve on (a) whether he is in breach of contract [and]{.underline} (b) the extent to which Rory will be successful in claiming damages [and]{.underline} (c) whether Rory is entitled to consider the contract at an end.

questions.description: Alison is a self-employed archaeologist. Company.
questions.text: Alison is a self-employed archaeologist. In January she sees a call for tenders for archaeological services in a national newspaper. The advertisement states that a private company, Modern Developments Ltd (MD Ltd), is looking for an archaeological survey of a piece of land over a two month period, and welcomes bids from all interested archaeologists by 30th January. The advertisement also states that "the lowest compliant bid will be accepted". Alison really wants the work, so she sends in her tender on time. Her tender states that she can provide the services for €20,000 "or €1,000 less than any lower bid". She is subsequently dismayed to learn that her tender was rejected. She later hears that the successful bidder offered to do the work for €19,000. She believes her bid was the lowest and wants to know if she has a cause of action against MD Ltd. In April she carries out an archaeological survey for Peter, a local businessman, for an agreed price of €10,000. Peter is to pay Alison by June 15th. However, by the end of June Peter has still not paid Alison for her services. Alison presses for payment, to no avail. Eventually in August Peter tells Alison that in the "current economic climate" he is not sure he will be able to pay her in full, but he offers her €6,000 in settlement of the debt. He tells her that if this offer is not accepted then nothing will be paid. Alison is worried about her cash flow, and reluctantly accepts Peter's offer. Peter pays Alison €6,000. A month later Alison changes her mind and looks for the balance, €4,000, from Peter. However, Peter says they had a binding agreement, and she cannot claim the €4,000. Moreover, he says that he relied on Alison's agreement to accept €6,000 and entered into other contracts in the knowledge that he would not have to pay Alison the balance. Advise Alison on her legal position vis à vis (a) MD Ltd [and]{.underline} (b) Peter.

questions.description: Explain what is meant by the rule of privity of contract [and]{.underline} discuss whether it is necessary to reform…
questions.text: Explain what is meant by the rule of privity of contract [and]{.underline} discuss whether it is necessary to reform the privity rule in Ireland.

questions.description: When will the Irish courts imply a term into a contract?
questions.text: When will the Irish courts imply a term into a contract? In your view, is this the correct approach to the implication of terms or should the courts be more (or less) willing to imply terms? Please note it is not necessary to discuss the implication of terms by statute or the Constitution in your answer.

questions.description: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the impact of EU directives and current harmonisation initiatives on Irish…
questions.text: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the impact of EU directives and current harmonisation initiatives on Irish contract law. Or (b) Discuss how exemption clauses are statutorily [and]{.underline} judicially controlled in Ireland.

questions.description: Discuss the approach of the Irish courts to a plea that a contract was entered into because of a common mistake of the…
questions.text: Discuss the approach of the Irish courts to a plea that a contract was entered into because of a common mistake of the contracting parties [and]{.underline} discuss whether equitable relief should be available for common mistake.
